dialogue.utterance: Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I was shot in my legs. I heard five shots at my door and went down. I feel really weak and have pain in my legs.
Are you pregnant?
Yes, I just found out I'm pregnant.
Have you ever had any medical issues or allergies?
No, nothing. I have no known allergies to medications or environments.
Are you aware of your surroundings and the time?
I think so. I'm a little tired.
You're alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. That's a GCS 15 (E4V5M6), consistent with A&Ox4/4.
You seem a bit slow to respond. How are you feeling now?
I just want to be still. I keep having to concentrate to stay awake.
Blood pressure is 48 over 21.
Pulse is 96 and very weak.
Respirations are 28 and fast.
Oxygen saturation is 82%, but your skin is cool, pale, and diaphoretic.
We'll check your glucose, but it may not be reliable due to the trauma setting.
Glucose result is suspect due to the trauma setting and is not being considered valid.
We applied a tourniquet to your left leg to stop the bleeding. Firefighters are holding it secure right now.
You have a penetrating wound to the left upper leg with significant active bleeding. We've controlled it with a tourniquet.
We've started intraosseous access in your right shoulder. Normal saline is the fluid being given.
After starting the fluids, blood pressure is now 120 over 48.
Pulse is 103 and still weak.
Respirations are 26 and fast. Placing you on a 4-lead ECG now.
Your EKG shows a wide complex tachycardia. We've noted this change since initiation of fluids.
Oxygen saturation is now 95%. You're being treated for significant blood loss. We may need to move you to the stretcher now.
On a scale from 0 to 10, how would you rate your pain right now?
It's 10, the worst pain I've ever felt.
Can you confirm again the events that led up to this?
Yes, I heard five shots, opened the door, got shot in both legs. I had no prior issues or injuries.
We activated general trauma management, protocol 4-1, for penetrating trauma - a gunshot wound to the leg. We applied a tourniquet and started an IO for fluid resuscitation.

Trauma team will manage further care. You were found in a supine position on the floor with no ID or contact info. You reported severe pain and low energy during treatment. You were initially seen as alert but now appearing more lethargic.
We are transporting to VCU Health Systems for a hot-transfer.
We will be using a stretcher; the tourniquet will be held secures by the firefighters during movement.
You have been prepared for transport to the hospital. I'll be with you the entire way.
We're doing a quick assessment for signs of bruising or scars on your skin.
No rashes or bruising other than the wound sites on the legs.
Are you having any neck or head pain, dizziness, or visual issues?
I'm lightheaded but no head pain.
Your mouth appears normal-no bruising or bleeding in the throat or ears.
Do you have chest pain or difficulty breathing?
Just shortness of breath, nothing in the chest.
Your abdomen is flat and not tender.
Do you have any pain when urinating or rectal bleeding?
No, I don't.
No obvious deformity or swelling noted on the non-injured limbs.
I'll check your blood pressure again now that we are starting the move.
Pulse remains at 103 and weak.
Respirations continue to be fast at 26.
Oxygen saturation is still 95%. We're maintaining oxygen as needed.
EKG tracing is repetitive tachycardia, no new changes.
We are continuing normal saline at a maintenance rate for fluid resuscitation.
The intraosseous site is secure and functioning well.
Your pain level still at 10?
Yes. It's hard to stay focused.
Are there any other additional injuries you feel anywhere else?
No, just the legs.
We'll be arriving at VCU shortly. Trauma team is aware of your condition.
The tourniquet will remain on your leg during transfer. The ER will take over from here.
Firefighters will help position you and ensure the tourniquet stays intact.
ER crew is at the door making final preparations for the ER wheelchair transfer.
We will transfer you immediately to the trauma nurses for further evaluation and care.
You now hand off to the hospital trauma team will include your current status, vitals, medications, and trauma protocol used.

The patient is a 32-year-old female with a gunshot wound to both upper legs, major blood loss, and is pregnant. Initial vitals were 48/21, P 96, and saturation 82%. Vitals improved with IO access and fluids.
The patient is alert and oriented but now lethargic. Treated under protocol 4-1 for general trauma management. Major controls are in place.
We've fully handed over patient care and all relevant medical documentation.
Your airway is clear and patent. Slight tachypnea observed but no signs of airway compromise.
No cyanosis or use of accessory muscles noted. Breathing is labored but consistent.
We rechecked peripheral pulses. No improvement at the radial. Still primarily relying on IO for access.
Your legs appear pale but no visible penetrating injury other than where we applied the tourniquet.
No neck injuries noted. No jugular vein distension. No lymph nodes visible.
No wounds to face or mouth. No bleeding from ears or nose.
Equal breath sounds, no adventitious-good bilateral expansion.
No masses or rigidity on the abdomen. Bowel sounds are normal at this stage.
No urine extravasation noted.
No fractures or dislocations observed on non-injured side. No vertebral abnormalities.
We are still on a wide complex tachycardia, but stable rhythm overall.
Patient is en route to ER with ongoing tachycardia and low peripheral perfusion. I'll be with you during the transfer.
Firefighters still on standby in case of any changes in leg wound or tourniquet position.
You're now being slid onto the stretcher and lifted. Try to stay still and focused.
Tourniquet remains tight. No further bleeding to report.
Condition remains stable, no need for immediate interventions en route.
Hospital team is ready for immediate transfer of care now. All resuscitative efforts stable.
Patient under heavy fluid resuscitation, expected to need trauma surgery and OB/gyn consultation for pregnancy.
That wraps up our field report and handoff. The patient is going to VCU under trauma and obstetrics protocol.
Airway still patent and no need for supplemental oxygen beyond what we've already provided.
Breathing still active, depth slightly reduced with ongoing exertion, but respiration rate improved.
No spontaneous cap refill. IO remains open and fluid flow ongoing.
No scarring pre-existing or rashes noticeable. Skin cool and still diaphoretic.
Slight eye lid edema possible otherwise normal head and eye reflexes.
Normal mucus membranes-no signs of trauma to the face or oral cavity.
No crackles. Equal chest expansion. No murmurs audible.
Gentle probing shows no tenderness or rigidity. No guarding observed.
No visible irregularities in the genitourinary tract. No discoloration in the area.
No swelling or tenderness in back or spine areas.
BP stable at 120/48. No signs of hypotension.
Pulse remains at 103, adequate for current status.
Respiratory rate still at 26. No signs of hypoventilation or hyperventilation.
Oxygen saturation sustained at 95%. Not dropping.
EKG tracing repeatable; same wide tachy as prior: No new EKG concerns.
We are ready to continue with resuscitation under hospital trauma and maternal care teams upon arrival.
